text_clean: This is an ANCIENT World Heritage Site. Don't expect everything to be spiffed up. The terraglyphs aren't all in perfect shape. Some of the oldest are 'deteriorated' because they're (like) ANCIENT, (man). Catch my drift? You'll meet every type of psycho-tourist and teeny-bopper wanna-be here. Some tour guides have their pet theories about the Nazca which they love to repeat over and over in really cool sunglasses. Want to know the main ideas researchers have about the lines and the people? There's a great movie that runs repeatedly at the airport for FREE. It tells the story of what is known along with some competing ideas that are disputed. Not everything is cut-and-dried. Book a flight with a reputable air transport company. Saving a few Soles on a taxi in town is great, but don't do that in the air. Research the Nazca and the airlines BEFORE you come. See the movie while you wait for your flight with a reputable company on your first day in Nazca, and get INSPIRED! This is an amazing place. One of the most amazing in the world. Enjoy it now before it's destroyed by so-called eco-tourism. NOTE: So your photos 'come out,' edit them by turning down the brightness and turning up the contrast. (AFTER the flight!) Postnote: I've read some of the reviews. These are not Incan; they are Nazcan, pre-Incan! If you went to Nazca, stood on a tower by the road, and complained you couldn't see anything, you're a fool. You have to go up in a plane. Skip breakfast! Some people posted the lines were made by aliens. Some posted they definately weren't. Question: How do you know? Go see. Post a description of what you see and learn. No one cares about your opinion. Also, Google has the lines blocked. I don't know why, but you have to actually GO THERE to see the lines. Don't post until you do.
label: positivo

I really don't think is original.

text_clean: Nice dining at the historical place. The food was good. Well cooked, plated, and served. The taste was exotic. We tried dishes made of local ingredients. Quinoa cheese ball was good. I liked it. The Guinea pig appetizer was so-so. The fatty part was little too chewy for me. If you like fried fatty food you may disagree on that. The fish was nice. I thought if the fish meat was little thinner, it would have tasted better with the quinoa crust. The green bean sauce was nice but again little too blend. I think it's a good place to try out. Staffs are friendly and saw many locals. Once was probably good for us travelling people.
label: positivo

text_clean: These ancient geoglyphs created by the pre incan civilizations (Paracas and Nazca) are a sight to behold! It's still shrouded in mystery. These elaborate depictions of different animals and insects like hummingbirds, monkeys and spiders still blows my mind. There's a variety in patterns and shapes. Some are simple lines going across the landscape, while others depict spirals, similar to crop circles. The scale of these geoglyphs are so huge that it's beauty can only be truly appreciated from the sky. If you're in Nazca I would recommend going to see them. The flight is not cheap but I think it's worth it. If you suffer from motion sickness, the flight over the nazca lines might surprise you. The plane has to tilt at a certain angle on either side so you can see the geoglyphs. At first it might seem like fun but after 20 minutes you might start feeling very sick. The flight is it's own experience. Try to snap a quick picture and face forward again. Apart from this, I still recommend doing this.

text_clean: If you come here I recommend camping. There's an entrance fee into the park, but the camping is free. You'll be greeted by donkeys, horses and cows who graze in the area and love the interaction. The views are spectacular at the campsite and the sky is usually clear at night which makes for perfect stargazing. There's a local family that lives up there that'll sell snacks and drinks at slightly higher prices than regular, but nothing too expensive. There's plenty of hiking and climbing to do there with spectacular views at every step. The hike to Laguna 69 is well worth the two hour hike. Take sunscreen though as the sun can get pretty brutal. Also, prepare for an hour+ of unpaved road to get there. Don't let that deter you this place is one of the best spots in Peru!
label: positivo

text_clean: Very Nice Park Huascarán National Park (Spanish: Parque Nacional Huascarán) is a Peruvian national park that comprises most of the mountain range known as Cordillera Blanca (the world's highest tropical mountain range) which is part of the central Andes, in the region of Ancash. The park covers an area of 340,000 ha (ca. 3.400 km2) and is managed by the Peruvian Network of Protected Natural Areas: SERNANP (Servicio Nacional de Áreas Naturales Protegidas). It was designated as a World Heritage Site in 1985 by UNESCO, is also a well-known mountaineering spot and harbors a unique biodiversity with plant species such as the Queen of the Andes, trees of the genera Polylepis and Buddleja, and animals such as spectacled bears, condors, vicunas and tarucas. The park is approximately 150 kilometres (93 mi) long from north to south and averages about 25 kilometres (16 mi) in width. The western slope of the Cordillera Blanca drains to the Pacific Ocean via the Santa River and the eastern slopes drain to the Marañon River, and ultimately to the Amazon River and the Atlantic Ocean. Thanks 😘

I did enjoy the hike up Machu Picchu mountain and the conversations with other travelers. We took the bus up, and hiked back down. The hike up is a serious climb, so be prepared! If you'd like to avoid the train, you can get a bus to a hydroelectric plant from Cusco and, from there, hike a couple hours to Aguas Calientes. Overall, the guides we heard didn't have knowledge that made it worth the cost. I'd recommend and audio tour online instead. There are more beautiful places and hikes to be found in the world.

text_clean: Incredible experience! Our guide, Jordan, was knowledgeable, and really tailored his tour to our interests. We took the Inca Bridge option, and was so pleased with how little people were on the trail. We got to see more of the orchids, insects ( especially the butterflies) and hummingbirds up close. The city of Machu Pichu was really impressive! At first it seemed like a lot of people, but by the time we got back from the Inca Bridge, most of them were gone. So it was not crowded. We were able to get stunning photos even in the low season! It did rain closer to the end of our tour, but not a lot, and we were prepared for it. Bring a rain jacket, and some waterproof pants, if you can. A hat also helps. That's if you're going during the rainy season. No umbrellas or selfie sticks are allowed.

text_clean: This is an ANCIENT World Heritage Site. Don't expect everything to be spiffed up. The terraglyphs aren't all in perfect shape. Some of the oldest are 'deteriorated' because they're (like) ANCIENT, (man). Catch my drift? You'll meet every type of psycho-tourist and teeny-bopper wanna-be here. Some tour guides have their pet theories about the Nazca which they love to repeat over and over in really cool sunglasses. Want to know the main ideas researchers have about the lines and the people? There's a great movie that runs repeatedly at the airport for FREE. It tells the story of what is known along with some competing ideas that are disputed. Not everything is cut-and-dried. Book a flight with a reputable air transport company. Saving a few Soles on a taxi in town is great, but don't do that in the air. Research the Nazca and the airlines BEFORE you come. See the movie while you wait for your flight with a reputable company on your first day in Nazca, and get INSPIRED! This is an amazing place. One of the most amazing in the world. Enjoy it now before it's destroyed by so-called eco-tourism. NOTE: So your photos 'come out,' edit them by turning down the brightness and turning up the contrast. (AFTER the flight!) Postnote: I've read some of the reviews. These are not Incan; they are Nazcan, pre-Incan! If you went to Nazca, stood on a tower by the road, and complained you couldn't see anything, you're a fool. You have to go up in a plane. Skip breakfast! Some people posted the lines were made by aliens. Some posted they definately weren't. Question: How do you know? Go see. Post a description of what you see and learn. No one cares about your opinion. Also, Google has the lines blocked. I don't know why, but you have to actually GO THERE to see the lines. Don't post until you do.
label: positivo

text_clean: These ancient geoglyphs created by the pre incan civilizations (Paracas and Nazca) are a sight to behold! It's still shrouded in mystery. These elaborate depictions of different animals and insects like hummingbirds, monkeys and spiders still blows my mind. There's a variety in patterns and shapes. Some are simple lines going across the landscape, while others depict spirals, similar to crop circles. The scale of these geoglyphs are so huge that it's beauty can only be truly appreciated from the sky. If you're in Nazca I would recommend going to see them. The flight is not cheap but I think it's worth it. If you suffer from motion sickness, the flight over the nazca lines might surprise you. The plane has to tilt at a certain angle on either side so you can see the geoglyphs. At first it might seem like fun but after 20 minutes you might start feeling very sick. The flight is it's own experience. Try to snap a quick picture and face forward again. Apart from this, I still recommend doing this.
label: neutro

text_clean: If you come here I recommend camping. There's an entrance fee into the park, but the camping is free. You'll be greeted by donkeys, horses and cows who graze in the area and love the interaction. The views are spectacular at the campsite and the sky is usually clear at night which makes for perfect stargazing. There's a local family that lives up there that'll sell snacks and drinks at slightly higher prices than regular, but nothing too expensive. There's plenty of hiking and climbing to do there with spectacular views at every step. The hike to Laguna 69 is well worth the two hour hike. Take sunscreen though as the sun can get pretty brutal. Also, prepare for an hour+ of unpaved road to get there. Don't let that deter you this place is one of the best spots in Peru!

I did enjoy the hike up Machu Picchu mountain and the conversations with other travelers. We took the bus up, and hiked back down. The hike up is a serious climb, so be prepared! If you'd like to avoid the train, you can get a bus to a hydroelectric plant from Cusco and, from there, hike a couple hours to Aguas Calientes. Overall, the guides we heard didn't have knowledge that made it worth the cost. I'd recommend and audio tour online instead. There are more beautiful places and hikes to be found in the world.

text_clean: This is an ANCIENT World Heritage Site. Don't expect everything to be spiffed up. The terraglyphs aren't all in perfect shape. Some of the oldest are 'deteriorated' because they're (like) ANCIENT, (man). Catch my drift? You'll meet every type of psycho-tourist and teeny-bopper wanna-be here. Some tour guides have their pet theories about the Nazca which they love to repeat over and over in really cool sunglasses. Want to know the main ideas researchers have about the lines and the people? There's a great movie that runs repeatedly at the airport for FREE. It tells the story of what is known along with some competing ideas that are disputed. Not everything is cut-and-dried. Book a flight with a reputable air transport company. Saving a few Soles on a taxi in town is great, but don't do that in the air. Research the Nazca and the airlines BEFORE you come. See the movie while you wait for your flight with a reputable company on your first day in Nazca, and get INSPIRED! This is an amazing place. One of the most amazing in the world. Enjoy it now before it's destroyed by so-called eco-tourism. NOTE: So your photos 'come out,' edit them by turning down the brightness and turning up the contrast. (AFTER the flight!) Postnote: I've read some of the reviews. These are not Incan; they are Nazcan, pre-Incan! If you went to Nazca, stood on a tower by the road, and complained you couldn't see anything, you're a fool. You have to go up in a plane. Skip breakfast! Some people posted the lines were made by aliens. Some posted they definately weren't. Question: How do you know? Go see. Post a description of what you see and learn. No one cares about your opinion. Also, Google has the lines blocked. I don't know why, but you have to actually GO THERE to see the lines. Don't post until you do.

text_clean: Es posible visualizar tres de las figuras desde el mirador instalado a la vera de la carretera Panamericana. Sus líneas son de unos 20 cm de ancho y el tamaño de las imágenes es fácilmente visible desde baja altura. El mantenimiento del sitio es escaso, con basura acumulada, baños en estado higiénico terrible, y tejido plástico que perturban la vista de la figura que se encuentra atravesada por la carretera. It is possible to view three of the figures from the viewpoint installed on the side of the Pan-American Highway. Its lines are about 20 cm wide and the size of the images is easily visible from low height. The maintenance of the site is scarce, with accumulated garbage, bathrooms in terrible hygienic state, and plastic fabric that disturb the view of the figure that is crossed by the road.

text_clean: If you come here I recommend camping. There's an entrance fee into the park, but the camping is free. You'll be greeted by donkeys, horses and cows who graze in the area and love the interaction. The views are spectacular at the campsite and the sky is usually clear at night which makes for perfect stargazing. There's a local family that lives up there that'll sell snacks and drinks at slightly higher prices than regular, but nothing too expensive. There's plenty of hiking and climbing to do there with spectacular views at every step. The hike to Laguna 69 is well worth the two hour hike. Take sunscreen though as the sun can get pretty brutal. Also, prepare for an hour+ of unpaved road to get there. Don't let that deter you this place is one of the best spots in Peru!
label: neutro

text_clean: Two stars compared to my expectations. It's a beautiful place, but overrun with people, expensive to get to, and v touristy. Think Disney meets Williamsburg. I did enjoy the hike up Machu Picchu mountain and the conversations with other travelers. We took the bus up, and hiked back down. The hike up is a serious climb, so be prepared! If you'd like to avoid the train, you can get a bus to a hydroelectric plant from Cusco and, from there, hike a couple hours to Aguas Calientes. Overall, the guides we heard didn't have knowledge that made it worth the cost. I'd recommend and audio tour online instead. There are more beautiful places and hikes to be found in the world.

text_clean: Incredible experience! Our guide, Jordan, was knowledgeable, and really tailored his tour to our interests. We took the Inca Bridge option, and was so pleased with how little people were on the trail. We got to see more of the orchids, insects ( especially the butterflies) and hummingbirds up close. The city of Machu Pichu was really impressive! At first it seemed like a lot of people, but by the time we got back from the Inca Bridge, most of them were gone. So it was not crowded. We were able to get stunning photos even in the low season! It did rain closer to the end of our tour, but not a lot, and we were prepared for it. Bring a rain jacket, and some waterproof pants, if you can. A hat also helps. That's if you're going during the rainy season. No umbrellas or selfie sticks are allowed.
label: neutro

text_clean: Very Nice Park Huascarán National Park (Spanish: Parque Nacional Huascarán) is a Peruvian national park that comprises most of the mountain range known as Cordillera Blanca (the world's highest tropical mountain range) which is part of the central Andes, in the region of Ancash. The park covers an area of 340,000 ha (ca. 3.400 km2) and is managed by the Peruvian Network of Protected Natural Areas: SERNANP (Servicio Nacional de Áreas Naturales Protegidas). It was designated as a World Heritage Site in 1985 by UNESCO, is also a well-known mountaineering spot and harbors a unique biodiversity with plant species such as the Queen of the Andes, trees of the genera Polylepis and Buddleja, and animals such as spectacled bears, condors, vicunas and tarucas. The park is approximately 150 kilometres (93 mi) long from north to south and averages about 25 kilometres (16 mi) in width. The western slope of the Cordillera Blanca drains to the Pacific Ocean via the Santa River and the eastern slopes drain to the Marañon River, and ultimately to the Amazon River and the Atlantic Ocean. Thanks 😘

text_clean: Incredible experience! Our guide, Jordan, was knowledgeable, and really tailored his tour to our interests. We took the Inca Bridge option, and was so pleased with how little people were on the trail. We got to see more of the orchids, insects ( especially the butterflies) and hummingbirds up close. The city of Machu Pichu was really impressive! At first it seemed like a lot of people, but by the time we got back from the Inca Bridge, most of them were gone. So it was not crowded. We were able to get stunning photos even in the low season! It did rain closer to the end of our tour, but not a lot, and we were prepared for it. Bring a rain jacket, and some waterproof pants, if you can. A hat also helps. That's if you're going during the rainy season. No umbrellas or selfie sticks are allowed.

We were able to get stunning photos even in the low season! It did rain closer to the end of our tour, but not a lot, and we were prepared for it. Bring a rain jacket, and some waterproof pants, if you can. A hat also helps. That's if you're going during the rainy season. No umbrellas or selfie sticks are allowed.

text_clean: This is an ANCIENT World Heritage Site. Don't expect everything to be spiffed up. The terraglyphs aren't all in perfect shape. Some of the oldest are 'deteriorated' because they're (like) ANCIENT, (man). Catch my drift? You'll meet every type of psycho-tourist and teeny-bopper wanna-be here. Some tour guides have their pet theories about the Nazca which they love to repeat over and over in really cool sunglasses. Want to know the main ideas researchers have about the lines and the people? There's a great movie that runs repeatedly at the airport for FREE. It tells the story of what is known along with some competing ideas that are disputed. Not everything is cut-and-dried. Book a flight with a reputable air transport company. Saving a few Soles on a taxi in town is great, but don't do that in the air. Research the Nazca and the airlines BEFORE you come. See the movie while you wait for your flight with a reputable company on your first day in Nazca, and get INSPIRED! This is an amazing place. One of the most amazing in the world. Enjoy it now before it's destroyed by so-called eco-tourism. NOTE: So your photos 'come out,' edit them by turning down the brightness and turning up the contrast. (AFTER the flight!) Postnote: I've read some of the reviews. These are not Incan; they are Nazcan, pre-Incan! If you went to Nazca, stood on a tower by the road, and complained you couldn't see anything, you're a fool. You have to go up in a plane. Skip breakfast! Some people posted the lines were made by aliens. Some posted they definately weren't. Question: How do you know? Go see. Post a description of what you see and learn. No one cares about your opinion. Also, Google has the lines blocked. I don't know why, but you have to actually GO THERE to see the lines. Don't post until you do.

text_clean: Nice dining at the historical place. The food was good. Well cooked, plated, and served. The taste was exotic. We tried dishes made of local ingredients. Quinoa cheese ball was good. I liked it. The Guinea pig appetizer was so-so. The fatty part was little too chewy for me. If you like fried fatty food you may disagree on that. The fish was nice. I thought if the fish meat was little thinner, it would have tasted better with the quinoa crust. The green bean sauce was nice but again little too blend. I think it's a good place to try out. Staffs are friendly and saw many locals. Once was probably good for us travelling people.

text_clean: If you come here I recommend camping. There's an entrance fee into the park, but the camping is free. You'll be greeted by donkeys, horses and cows who graze in the area and love the interaction. The views are spectacular at the campsite and the sky is usually clear at night which makes for perfect stargazing. There's a local family that lives up there that'll sell snacks and drinks at slightly higher prices than regular, but nothing too expensive. There's plenty of hiking and climbing to do there with spectacular views at every step. The hike to Laguna 69 is well worth the two hour hike. Take sunscreen though as the sun can get pretty brutal. Also, prepare for an hour+ of unpaved road to get there. Don't let that deter you this place is one of the best spots in Peru!

text_clean: La visita es muy bonita, los paisajes del cañon son espectaculares. Paras a ver y hacerte fotos con alpacas y llamas, que es lo que mas nos gustó. El mirador cruz del condor también muy bonito, y mas si ves alguno volando. El mirador de volcanes también es impresionante, aunque exigente porque te encuentras a 4900m sobre el nivel del mar, te puedes marear fácilmente. Te dan de desayunar al comienzo, y al final, el almuerzo hay que pagárselo, un bufet bueno. Lo único malo es que recogen a las 02:30-03:00, demasiado temprano, y el camino es 3h y media ir, 3h y media volver, por caminos de montaña. Ahora bien, la visita es obligada.

text_clean: If you come here I recommend camping. There's an entrance fee into the park, but the camping is free. There's a local family that lives up there that'll sell snacks and drinks at slightly higher prices than regular, but nothing too expensive. There's plenty of hiking and climbing to do there with spectacular views at every step. The hike to Laguna 69 is well worth the two hour hike. Take sunscreen though as the sun can get pretty brutal. Also, prepare for an hour+ of unpaved road to get there. Don't let that deter you this place is one of the best spots in Peru!
